# Knowledge Placement

## The Physics

A pretrained AI model can be adapted to a specific task in three places. Each place holds knowledge differently, with different cost and durability:

- **Prompt** — instructions and context provided at inference. Immediate, ephemeral, reset every turn. Cheapest to write, gone the moment the conversation ends.
- **Retrieval (RAG)** — an external store the model reads at inference. Knowledge lives in editable source files. Change the source, change the answer, no retraining required.
- **Weights (fine-tuning)** — the model's internal parameters, modified by additional training. Knowledge becomes intrinsic to the model. Fast at inference, rigid to update, expensive to reverse.

The chef analogy makes it concrete. Imagine hiring a chef trained at the world's best culinary school — broadly skilled, knows ten thousand techniques. Three ways to teach them your restaurant's recipes:

- **Prompting** is yelling instructions over their shoulder while they cook. Cheap and immediate, but every dish requires fresh instruction. They never internalize your style.
- **RAG** is giving them a binder of recipes to consult while cooking. Knowledge lives outside the chef in something you can edit any time. Change a recipe in the binder, the next dish reflects it.
- **Fine-tuning** is putting them through a six-month apprenticeship at your restaurant. After it, your house style is intrinsic — they cook your way without checking the binder. Updating a recipe means another apprenticeship, and they may forget some general techniques in the process.

The placement choice depends on three properties of the knowledge: how often it changes (volatility), how broadly it applies (scope), and how much it should shape default behavior versus respond to per-turn input. Volatile knowledge belongs in retrieval. Stable behavior belongs in weights. Per-turn context belongs in the prompt. Wrong placement is structurally wasteful — putting volatile knowledge in weights creates a model that lies six months later with no quick fix.

## Why It Matters

- **Wrong placement compounds waste.** Volatile knowledge in weights forces full retraining cycles to update single facts. Stable behavior re-described in every prompt wastes tokens and invites drift.
- **Right placement compounds leverage.** Stable behavior in weights produces a model that defaults correctly without reminders. Volatile knowledge in retrieval can be edited in seconds. Per-turn context stays surgical.
- **Cost asymmetry is large.** Prompt edits are free. Retrieval edits are cheap (edit a source file, re-embed). Weight edits are expensive (collect dataset, run training, validate, deploy). A correct placement decision compounds; a wrong one taxes every future change.
- **Auditability differs by placement.** Knowledge in retrieval traces back to source files with provenance. Knowledge in weights is opaque — you cannot point at a specific weight and explain why it produces a specific output. Compliance and trust requirements push knowledge toward retrieval by default.
- **Occasional fine-tuning is correct, not always-RAG.** Fine-tuning is the right tool for stable behaviors the system embodies — output formats, classification tasks, structural patterns. The error is treating fine-tuning as a knowledge-store substitute for retrieval.

## Academic/Industry Backing

- Retrieval-Augmented Generation literature (Lewis et al., 2020; SOPRAG and GraphRAG advances 2024–2026): retrieval consistently outperforms fine-tuning for knowledge that changes.
- Catastrophic forgetting in continual learning (McCloskey & Cohen, 1989; active research through present): repeated fine-tuning erodes prior capabilities — evidence against using weights as a primary knowledge store.
- Modern parameter-efficient fine-tuning practice (LoRA, QLoRA, instruction tuning 2023–2026): behavioral fine-tuning at modest data volumes (100–10K examples) reliably succeeds at format and style adaptation; pure knowledge-encoding fine-tuning at the same volumes does not.

## Connected Objects

- JOS-C07 (Knowledge Decay) — placing volatile knowledge in weights accelerates decay because the cost to refresh is high; the chef forgets last quarter's specials before you can re-train them.
- JOS-C08 (Compression Gradient) — fine-tuning is itself a compression operation: text becomes weight patterns. The placement decision is a compression-gradient choice — recent and volatile stays high-resolution (prompt/retrieval), stable and structural compresses into weights.
- JOS-C04 (Configuration Density) — prompt, retrieval, and weights form a configuration-density spectrum. Prompt is highest density per token (and lowest persistence). Weights are lowest density per parameter (and highest persistence).
